content.text: Migration from Blueprints

Azure Blueprints retired July 11, 2026. Existing assignments continue to show in the portal but lose lock enforcement, DINE policy effects stop deploying, and no new assignments can be created. Complete migration before this date.

Migrating from Azure Blueprints to Deployment Stacks requires mapping each blueprint construct to its Deployment Stack equivalent: blueprint definitions become Bicep templates, ARM template artifacts become Bicep modules, resource group artifacts become inline resource group declarations in Bicep, policy assignment artifacts become standalone az policy assignment create calls or ALZ-Bicep policy assignments, and role assignment artifacts become roleAssignment resources in Bicep. Blueprint lock modes map to Deployment Stack denySettings (AllResourcesDoNotDelete → DenyDelete; AllResourcesReadOnly → DenyWriteAndDelete). The critical migration insight is that a Deployment Stack can take ownership of resources already deployed by a blueprint — the stack does not need to redeploy them.

attrs.alt: Blueprint to Deployment Stack migration map: construct equivalents and six-step migration process

content.text: Step 1: Inventory Assignments

Before migrating, enumerate every blueprint assignment across all subscriptions in the tenant using az graph query on BlueprintResources. Export the assignment details (version, lock mode, parameter values, resource group mappings) and the artifact definitions (particularly the ARM template content from each template artifact) to JSON files. This inventory drives the Bicep conversion work in step 3 and ensures no subscription is missed.

# Step 1: Inventory all blueprint assignments across the tenant
# Run this before starting migration to understand scope.

# List assignments across all subscriptions you have access to
az graph query -q "
  BlueprintResources
  | where type =~ 'microsoft.blueprint/blueprintassignments'
  | extend
    bpId = tostring(properties.blueprintId),
    lockMode = tostring(properties.locks.mode),
    state = tostring(properties.provisioningState)
  | parse bpId with * '/blueprints/' bpName '/versions/' bpVersion
  | project subscriptionId, assignmentName=name, bpName, bpVersion, lockMode, state
  | order by subscriptionId, assignmentName asc
" --output table

# Export full assignment details for each subscription to JSON files
while IFS= read -r SUB_ID; do
  echo "Exporting assignments for $SUB_ID"
  az blueprint assignment list \
    --subscription "$SUB_ID" \
    -o json > "assignments_${SUB_ID}.json"
done < subscriptions.txt

# Extract the artifact definitions from each blueprint definition
MG_ID='alz-landing-zones'
BP_NAME='landing-zone-baseline'

az blueprint artifact list \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  -o json > blueprint_artifacts.json

# Export each ARM template artifact's template JSON
az blueprint artifact show \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  --artifact-name 'key-vault' \
  --query 'template' -o json > artifact_key_vault.json
Step 2-3: Export and Convert ARM Artifacts

Export each ARM template artifact's template JSON using az blueprint artifact show --query 'template'. Convert each JSON to Bicep using az bicep decompile. The decompiled output requires cleanup: replace concat() expressions with Bicep string interpolation, replace [parameters('x')] with Bicep param declarations, and replace resourceId() calls with Bicep resource symbolic name references. Validate the converted Bicep with az bicep build before proceeding.

# Step 2-3: Export ARM template artifacts and convert to Bicep

# Export the ARM template from a blueprint artifact
az blueprint artifact show \
  --blueprint-name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --management-group 'alz-landing-zones' \
  --artifact-name 'key-vault' \
  --query 'template' -o json > key_vault_template.json

# Convert ARM to Bicep
az bicep decompile --file key_vault_template.json
# Produces key_vault_template.bicep — review the output carefully.
# Common decompile issues to fix manually:
#   - Replace concat() with string interpolation: '${varName}-suffix'
#   - Replace [parameters('x')] with param x string
#   - Replace resourceId() calls with resource symbolic name references

# Example: cleaned-up Bicep from a Key Vault ARM artifact
cat << 'EOF' > key_vault.bicep
param location string = resourceGroup().location
param keyVaultAdminObjectId string

@description('Key Vault for workload secrets')
resource keyVault 'Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults@2023-02-01' = {
  name: 'kv-${uniqueString(resourceGroup().id)}'
  location: location
  properties: {
    sku: { family: 'A', name: 'standard' }
    tenantId: tenant().tenantId
    enableRbacAuthorization: true
    enableSoftDelete: true
    softDeleteRetentionInDays: 90
    enablePurgeProtection: true
    networkAcls: { defaultAction: 'Deny', bypass: 'AzureServices', ipRules: [], virtualNetworkRules: [] }
  }
}
EOF

# Validate the converted Bicep
az bicep build --file key_vault.bicep
Step 4: Create the Deployment Stack

Create the Deployment Stack targeting the same subscription with the converted Bicep template. Use --action-on-unmanage detachAll so resources persist if the stack is ever deleted (matching blueprint assignment delete behavior). The stack will import existing resources that match the template's resource declarations — no redeployment occurs for already-deployed resources. The denySettings mode controls write/delete protection analogous to the blueprint lock mode. Use --deny-settings-excluded-principals to exempt break-glass accounts from the deny policy.

# Step 4: Create a Deployment Stack to replace a blueprint assignment
# The stack manages the same resources that were deployed by the blueprint.
# Run BEFORE deleting the blueprint assignment — the stack takes ownership
# of existing resources without redeploying them.

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'

# Option A: Subscription-scoped stack (equivalent to blueprint with targetScope: subscription)
az stack sub create \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline-stack' \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file landing_zone_baseline.bicep \
  --parameters subscriptionLocation=eastus \
              logAnalyticsWorkspaceId='/subscriptions/<mgmt-sub>/resourceGroups/rg-management-prod-eastus/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/log-management-prod-eastus' \
              appTeamGroupObjectId='<app-team-oid>' \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
  --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll
  # action-on-unmanage detachAll = resources persist if stack is deleted (matches blueprint behavior)
  # action-on-unmanage deleteAll = resources deleted if stack is deleted (stricter than blueprint)

# Option B: Management-group-scoped stack (for subscriptions managed across a whole MG)
az stack mg create \
  --name 'platform-governance-stack' \
  --location eastus \
  --management-group-id 'alz-landing-zones' \
  --template-file platform_governance.bicep \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyWriteAndDelete \
  --deny-settings-excluded-principals '<break-glass-account-oid>' \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll

# Show the stack and its managed resources
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline-stack' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query '{Status:provisioningState, DenyMode:denySettings.mode, ResourceCount:length(resources)}' \
  -o json
Updating a Deployment Stack

After migration, Deployment Stacks replace blueprint assignment updates. Run az stack sub set with an updated Bicep file to apply changes — the command is idempotent and only touches resources that differ from the new template. Use az stack sub validate first for a what-if preview of changes. Stack versioning uses git tags on the Bicep file rather than an explicit version number like Blueprint's publish system. Tag the Bicep file with v1.1 in git after each update.

# Updating a Deployment Stack (equivalent to blueprint version update)
# Stack updates are idempotent — unchanged resources are not redeployed.

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'

# Update the stack with a new Bicep file version
az stack sub set \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline-stack' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file landing_zone_baseline_v2.bicep \
  --parameters subscriptionLocation=eastus \
              appTeamGroupObjectId='<app-team-oid>' \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
  --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll

# What-if before updating (preview changes without applying)
az stack sub validate \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline-stack' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file landing_zone_baseline_v2.bicep \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete

# List managed resources in the stack after update
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline-stack' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query 'resources[].{Id:id, Status:provisioningState}' \
  -o table
Step 5-6: Validate and Remove Blueprint Assignment

Before deleting the blueprint assignment, verify the Deployment Stack manages all expected resources and that denySettings are active. Test the deny protection by attempting to delete a managed resource — it should fail with a RequestDisallowedByPolicy error. Only after the stack is validated should you delete the blueprint assignment. Deleting the assignment removes the blueprint lock mode (which is now redundant since the stack's denySettings provide the same protection) and the assignment object.

# Step 5-6: Validate the stack, then remove the blueprint assignment

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'

# Validate the stack manages all expected resources
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline-stack' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query 'resources[].id' -o tsv | sort

# Verify denySettings are active (equivalent to blueprint lock mode)
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline-stack' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query 'denySettings' -o json

# Attempt a delete on a managed resource — should fail with denySettings error
# az group delete --name 'rg-payments-prod-eastus' --yes
# Error: Code: RequestDisallowedByPolicy

# Once validated, delete the blueprint assignment
# (resources stay; only the assignment object and locks are removed)
az blueprint assignment delete \
  --name 'assign-landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --yes

# Verify no blueprint assignment remains
az blueprint assignment list --subscription $TARGET_SUB -o table 2>&1

# Verify resources are still present (not deleted by assignment removal)
az group list --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Location:location, State:properties.provisioningState}' \
  -o table
Blueprint to Deployment Stack Feature Mapping
Blueprint Feature
Deployment Stack Equivalent
Notes
Blueprint definition (container)
Bicep file + parameters file in git
Use git tags for version tracking instead of az blueprint publish
resourceGroup artifact
resource group{} block in Bicep or targetScope: resourceGroup
Bicep handles resource group creation inline; no separate placeholder needed
ARM template artifact
Bicep module (module keyword) or resource declarations
Use az bicep decompile to convert existing ARM artifacts
Policy assignment artifact
Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments resource in Bicep or az policy assignment create
DINE policies: add system-assigned identity on the policyAssignment resource
Role assignment artifact
Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments resource in Bicep
Same resource type, same principalType requirement
AllResourcesDoNotDelete lock
--deny-settings-mode DenyDelete
Prevents delete; allows writes (config changes)
AllResourcesReadOnly lock
--deny-settings-mode DenyWriteAndDelete
Prevents all writes and deletes
Assignment parameter values
Bicep parameters file (*.bicepparam or JSON)
Store per-subscription params in separate .bicepparam files
Assignment identity (SystemAssigned)
Stack uses the calling user/SP identity
No separate identity — the user/SP running az stack sub create needs appropriate permissions
Assignment update to new version
az stack sub set with updated Bicep file
Same idempotent pattern; stack tracks managed resources and updates only changed ones
Migrating Policy Assignment Artifacts

Policy assignment artifacts from blueprints become standalone Azure Policy assignments after migration. Audit and Deny-effect assignments translate directly to az policy assignment create without any identity. DeployIfNotExists (DINE) assignments require a system-assigned managed identity (--mi-system-assigned) and a role assignment granting the identity Contributor on the subscription — the same requirement that existed in the blueprint artifact. DINE policies still require a remediation task after assignment, regardless of whether they were deployed by a blueprint or directly.

# Migrating Blueprint policy assignment artifacts to standalone policy assignments
# Blueprint policy assignments are subscription-scoped; keep the same scope after migration.

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'
LOG_WS_ID='/subscriptions/<mgmt-sub>/resourceGroups/rg-management-prod-eastus/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/log-management-prod-eastus'

# Get current blueprint policy assignment details
az blueprint artifact show \
  --blueprint-name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --management-group 'alz-landing-zones' \
  --artifact-name 'require-https-storage' \
  --query '{PolicyId:policyDefinitionId,Params:parameters}' -o json

# Re-create the policy assignment outside the blueprint
# Use the same policy definition ID and parameter values
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'require-https-storage' \
  --display-name 'Storage accounts should use HTTPS transfer' \
  --policy '404c3081-a854-4457-ae30-26a93ef643f9' \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$TARGET_SUB" \
  --params '{}'

# DINE policy: also needs system-assigned identity and role assignment
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deploy-diag-storage' \
  --display-name 'Deploy diagnostic settings for Storage Accounts' \
  --policy '59759c62-9a22-4cdf-ae64-074495983fef' \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$TARGET_SUB" \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus \
  --params "{\"logAnalyticsWorkspace\":{\"value\":\"$LOG_WS_ID\"}}"

# Grant the DINE policy identity Monitoring Contributor on the subscription
POLICY_OID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name 'deploy-diag-storage' \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$TARGET_SUB" \
  --query identity.principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Contributor' \
  --assignee-object-id $POLICY_OID \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$TARGET_SUB"

# Trigger remediation (DINE doesn't retroactively apply — same as blueprint)
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'remediate-diag-storage' \
  --policy-assignment 'deploy-diag-storage' \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$TARGET_SUB"
Complete Bicep Template Replacing a Blueprint

A single subscription-scoped Bicep file replaces the combination of resource group artifacts, ARM template artifacts, and role assignment artifacts from a blueprint definition. The targetScope = 'subscription' enables inline resource group declarations. Blueprint RG placeholders become resource group declarations. ARM template artifacts become Bicep modules referencing the converted .bicep files. Role assignment artifacts become Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments resources at subscription scope. This file becomes the source of truth — commit it to git and use git tags to track versions.
Common Migration Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Deleting the blueprint assignment before creating the Deployment Stack
Resources lose blueprint lock protection during the gap; in high-security environments, resources can be modified or deleted by users with RBAC access
Create the Deployment Stack first; validate denySettings; then delete the blueprint assignment
Using --action-on-unmanage deleteAll on the first migration attempt
If the stack parameters are wrong or the template has errors, resources are deleted when the failing stack is cleaned up
Always start with detachAll during migration; switch to deleteAll only for net-new stacks where auto-cleanup is intentional
Not migrating DINE policy artifacts — only moving ARM template artifacts
DINE effects stop deploying after blueprint retirement; existing resources accumulate compliance drift over time
Recreate every DINE policy artifact as a standalone policy assignment with managed identity and a remediation task
Omitting policy assignments from the Deployment Stack Bicep
Policy assignments are unmanaged after blueprint retirement and can be manually deleted without the stack preventing it
Include Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments resources in the Bicep template so the stack tracks and denies deletion of policy assignments
Converting ARM artifacts using only az bicep decompile without cleanup
Decompiled Bicep uses deprecated API versions, unsafe string concat patterns, and missing best practices like purge protection
Review every decompiled file: update API versions, add missing security properties (softDelete, purgeProtection, TLS minimum version), clean up concat expressions
Not updating subscription CSV after migration
The pipeline re-runs blueprint assignment create against already-migrated subscriptions (if the pipeline still references blueprint CLI)
Update the onboarding pipeline to use az stack sub create instead of az blueprint assignment create before the retirement date
Summary

Migrate from Azure Blueprints to Deployment Stacks before the July 11, 2026 retirement date. The six-step process: inventory all assignments with Resource Graph, export ARM template artifacts, convert to Bicep, create the Deployment Stack (which imports existing resources without redeployment), validate stack management and denySettings, then delete the blueprint assignment. Blueprint lock modes map to denySettings (DoNotDelete → DenyDelete; ReadOnly → DenyWriteAndDelete). Use --action-on-unmanage detachAll to match blueprint assignment delete behavior (resources persist when stack is deleted).
